questions.text: I find that I can attend emotionally intense events — funerals, weddings, farewells — without my own system becoming turbulent.
I tend to allocate the majority of my resources to my own development.
I keep working when I'm energized regardless of what my schedule says, and rest when the energy fades.
I find that my inclination to approach emotionally loaded situations with analytical rigor has protected me from the regret that comes with impulsive, emotionally driven decisions.
I experience the ups and downs of life as gentle undulations rather than dramatic swings.
I notice that my entire physiological state shifts when I walk into a tense situation.
I make lists for grocery shopping, packing, and other routine tasks.
I believe that the capacity to examine human hardship with intellectual precision is what makes systemic responses to suffering possible.
I do not automatically read into people's tone or body language.
I allocate my energy deliberately, doing demanding work when I'm sharpest.
I find that taking care of others' needs before my own feels right.
I hold the view that personal rituals and routines are sacred and should not be disrupted by others' requests.
I feel that my emotional reactions often have a life of their own, beyond my conscious control.
I spend my time without tracking it, trusting my sense of how the day went.
I believe that the connections I build should create value for others, not just for me.
I feel that the obligation to respond to suffering does not diminish with distance — a stranger's pain matters as much as a friend's.
I believe that self-effacement is a strength, not a weakness.
I invest time creating systems that make future work easier even if setup takes a while.
I find that my default mode is steady contentment rather than restless searching or anticipation.
I value my ability to remain objective when those around me are in emotional distress.
I prefer to collect others' ideas and find the common thread.
I sense when a conversation partner is mirroring my energy not out of genuine rapport but as a social technique.
I proactively recruit people to my initiatives.
I notice when a group instinctively leaves a chair empty next to someone they find socially difficult.
I instinctively adapt to whatever structure the group has established.
I find that I often need context explained that others seem to absorb from the social atmosphere.
I naturally assume the authority to speak on behalf of a group.
I adjust naturally as I go rather than setting aside formal review periods.
I tend not to register when a person volunteers less information than usual because of who is present.
My reactions arrive before my thoughts do — my body knows what I feel before my mind catches up.
I feel that the obligation to act on behalf of someone who is suffering is among the clearest moral intuitions I have.
I see group work as an opportunity to shape people's priorities.
I hold the view that every person's suffering, no matter how small it appears from the outside, deserves to be taken seriously.
I feel that my personal comfort and satisfaction are things I should actively cultivate.
I am highly aware of who is making eye contact with whom in a group.
I enjoy helping the group name the problem together and co-create a solution.
